---
title: "Building Slides with Quarto"
subtitle: "A hands-on teaching session"
author: "Your Name"
institute: "Université Mohammed VI des Sciences et de la Santé"
date: last-modified
format:
  beamer:
    theme: default
    colortheme: default
    aspectratio: 169
    navigation: horizontal
    include-in-header: slides-in-header.tex
execute:
  echo: false
bibliography: example.bib
---

## Welcome

This session teaches how to build a slide deck with Quarto, one concept
per slide. Every feature shown is demonstrated live: run
`quarto render quarto-slides-course.qmd` and follow along in the source.

## Session outline

1. Getting started
2. Text and structure
3. Images and figures
4. Code and its output
5. Tables
6. Layout and emphasis
7. Citations and cross-references
8. Rendering and publishing
9. Using this for your own defense

# Getting started

## What is Quarto

- An open-source publishing system built on Pandoc.
- One plain-text `.qmd` file compiles to slides, articles, books, or
  websites.
- Combines Markdown, executable code (R, Python, Julia, Observable), and a
  YAML header that configures the whole document.

## Quarto slide formats

Quarto's `format:` key decides what a `.qmd` renders to. For slides, the
three options are:

- **revealjs**: HTML slides, run in any browser, easy to embed video/JS.
- **beamer**: LaTeX/PDF slides (used by this course and the thesis
  defense deck), best when you need print-quality PDF output.
- **pptx**: editable PowerPoint, useful when collaborators need to edit
  slides without Quarto installed.

## Anatomy of a `.qmd` file

Three parts, always in this order:

1. A YAML header between `---` fences: title, author, format, execution
   options.
2. Markdown content, with `##` (level-2 heading) starting a new slide.
3. Optional executable code chunks, fenced with triple backticks.

## The YAML header

```markdown
---
title: "My Talk"
author: "Your Name"
format: beamer
---
```

- `title` / `subtitle` / `author` / `date` populate the title slide.
- `format` chooses the output type (`beamer`, `revealjs`, `pptx`, ...).
- Format-specific options nest under the format name, e.g.
  `format: {beamer: {aspectratio: 169}}`.

## Slides are just level-2 headings

```markdown
## First slide

Content here.

## Second slide

More content.
```

Every `##` starts a new slide automatically; no explicit slide separators
needed.

# Text and structure

## Headings inside a slide

A level-2 heading (`##`) makes a new slide; level-3 (`###`) and below
create subheadings *within* the current slide, useful for grouping
content without splitting it across slides.

## Lists and emphasis

Standard Markdown works as expected:

- `**bold**`, `*italic*`, `` `code` ``
- `-` for bullets, `1.` for numbered lists
- Nested lists by indenting two spaces

1. First step
2. Second step
   - a sub-point
   - another sub-point

## Speaker notes

Wrap presenter-only text in a `{.notes}` div. It compiles to `\note{...}`
in beamer and to hidden speaker notes in revealjs: visible to the
presenter, not the audience:

```markdown
::: {.notes}
Remind the audience why this matters before moving on.
:::
```

::: {.notes}
This is an actual speaker note attached to this slide.
:::

# Images and figures

## Insert a static image

Plain Markdown image syntax works in every Quarto format, with an
optional caption and size attribute:

```markdown
![UM6SS logo](images/logo.png){width="30%"}
```

![UM6SS logo](images/logo.png){width="30%" fig-align="center"}

## Insert a figure from code

A labeled, captioned code chunk produces a numbered, cross-referenceable
figure instead of a static file:

```{{r}}
#| label: fig-example
#| fig-cap: "Caption used for cross-references"
plot(1:10)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-example
#| fig-cap: "Caption used for cross-references"
plot(1:10)
```

## Resize figures at render time

`fig-width` / `fig-height` (in inches) control the plotting device
*before* the image is generated: this changes proportions and text size,
not just the final display size:

```{r}
#| label: fig-resized
#| fig-cap: "Generated at a smaller device size."
#| fig-width: 4
#| fig-height: 2.6
plot(1:10, type = "l")
```

## Resize the displayed output

`out-width` scales the *already generated* image inside the document,
without changing the plotting device: the same plot as before, just
displayed smaller:

```{r}
#| label: fig-outwidth
#| fig-cap: "Same plot, displayed smaller via out-width."
#| fig-width: 4
#| fig-height: 2.6
#| out-width: "35%"
plot(1:10, type = "l")
```

# Code and its output

## Display code, not just output

`echo: true` shows the chunk source above its result: essential when
teaching, usually turned off for results-only slides:

```{{r}}
#| echo: true
head(mtcars, 3)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
head(mtcars, 3)
```

## Controlling what a chunk shows

Common chunk options, set per-chunk with `#| option: value`:

- `echo`: show/hide the source code.
- `eval`: run/skip the code entirely.
- `warning` / `message`: hide noisy console output.
- `include`: run the code but show nothing (useful for `source()` calls).

```{r}
#| label: setup-quiet
#| include: false
set.seed(1)
```

## Sourcing shared code

A `source()` call with `include: false` loads reusable functions without
printing anything, exactly how this course's companion thesis defense
deck reuses `R/shared-results.R` so the manuscript and the slides never
show different numbers.

```{{r}}
#| include: false
source("R/shared-results.R")
```

# Tables

## Tables from code

`knitr::kable()` turns any data frame into a formatted table, labeled the
same way as a figure:

```{r}
#| label: tbl-example
#| tbl-cap: "A simple summary table."
knitr::kable(head(mtcars[, 1:4], 3))
```

## Markdown tables

For small, static tables, plain Markdown pipe syntax is often faster than
writing code:

```markdown
| Item          | Status      |
|---------------|-------------|
| Data          | Complete    |
| Analysis      | In progress |
```

| Item          | Status      |
|---------------|-------------|
| Data          | Complete    |
| Analysis      | In progress |

# Layout and emphasis

## Incremental reveals

Wrap a list in `{.incremental}` to step through points one at a time
instead of showing them all at once:

::: {.incremental}
- First point appears on click.
- Then this one.
- Then this one.
:::

## Two-column layouts

`columns` / `column` divs place content side by side: a figure next to
explanatory text is the most common use:

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Left column**

- Method
- Sample size
- Key assumption
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| label: fig-column-example
#| fig-cap: "A figure placed beside text."
plot(1:10, type = "b")
```
:::
:::

## Callout blocks

Callouts (`{.callout-note}`, `-warning`, `-important`, `-tip`) draw the
eye to a key message. In beamer they render as a colored block:

```markdown
::: {.callout-important}
The primary endpoint must be pre-registered before unblinding.
:::
```

::: {.callout-important}
The primary endpoint must be pre-registered before unblinding.
:::

# Citations and cross-references

## Citations

Citations use `[@key]`, pulling from a `.bib` file declared with
`bibliography:` in the YAML header: the same syntax and the same file
work in a Quarto book, article, or slide deck:

A reporting standard such as CONSORT [@Moher2010] or STROBE [@VonElm2007]
can be cited on a slide exactly as it would be in a manuscript.

## Cross-references

Anything with a `label: fig-...`, `tbl-...`, or `sec-...` can be
referenced with `@label` instead of a hardcoded number:

- `@fig-example` -> "Figure 1" (auto-numbered, auto-updated).
- `@tbl-example` -> "Table 1".
- Works identically across the thesis PDF and the slide deck, so
  renumbering a figure never breaks a reference.

# Rendering and publishing

## One script, several formats

The same `.qmd` can target a different output by changing only the
`format:` key; the Markdown body, code chunks, and citations stay
unchanged:

```yaml
format: revealjs   # or: beamer, pptx
```

## Commands you will use constantly

- `quarto render file.qmd`: render once.
- `quarto preview file.qmd`: live reload while editing.
- `quarto render file.qmd --to pptx`: try a different format without
  touching the YAML.
- `quarto render file.qmd --to beamer -M keep-tex:true`: keep the
  intermediate `.tex` file, useful for debugging LaTeX errors.

# Using this for your own defense

## Start from `slides.qmd`, not a blank file

This repository already has a working UM6SS-branded deck at `slides.qmd`.
For your own defense:

1. Copy or edit `slides.qmd` directly; keep the YAML header as-is so the
   branding and format stay UM6SS-consistent.
2. Update `title`, `subtitle`, `author`, and `institute` in the header.
3. Replace the placeholder slides (Overview, Presentation flow, Results)
   with your own research context, objectives, methods, and results.

## Reuse figures from your thesis chapters

Do not copy-paste a plot from your manuscript into the slide deck. Follow
the pattern already set up in this repository:

1. Put your analysis code in `R/shared-results.R` as a function.
2. Call that function from a labeled chunk in your `Chapters/*.qmd` file.
3. Call the *same* function from a chunk in `slides.qmd`.

The figure and the number on your slide will then always match what is
printed in the manuscript, even after you rerun the analysis.

## Checklist before presenting

::: {.incremental}
- Render both documents after any change: `quarto render` and
  `quarto render slides.qmd`.
- Confirm the numbers on your results slide match `Chapters/Chapter2.qmd`.
- Check the title slide: your name, supervisor, and institute are correct.
- Time yourself; trim slides rather than shrinking font size to fit more
  content.
:::

## Recap

::: {.incremental}
- A `.qmd` file is YAML + Markdown + code chunks.
- `##` starts a new slide.
- Figures and tables can come from code or from static files.
- `fig-width`/`fig-height` change the plot; `out-width` changes the
  display size.
- Citations, cross-references, and shared R scripts keep a deck and its
  companion manuscript consistent.
:::

## References

::: {#refs}
:::

## Closing

Thank you. Now build your own deck.
